games.matchupSummary: The Mets open a three-game home series against Philadelphia riding back-to-back wins and a rotation that lines up well for this spot.

mets.name: Kodai Senga
mets.note: ERA inflated over last three starts; FIP and xERA suggest underlying performance remains strong.

opp.name: Zack Wheeler
opp.note: Consistent but hittable at Citi Field historically.

sections.heading: Offensive Matchup
sections.body: The Mets carry a wRC+ of 112 against right-handed pitching this season, sitting above the league average and ahead of Philadelphia's 104 in the same split. Their hard-hit rate and barrel percentage are both slightly higher, and the lineup has been consistent over the past two weeks with most of the top five carrying OPS numbers north of .870. Wheeler is a legitimate arm, but this Mets offense has the profile to work counts and generate damage.

sections.heading: Pitching and Bullpen
sections.body: Senga's ERA over his last three starts reads higher than his actual performance warrants. His FIP sits at 3.12 over that same stretch and his strikeout-to-walk profile has not degraded - the surface numbers are inflated by a handful of hard-contact innings that do not reflect the underlying quality of his starts. Meanwhile, the Phillies bullpen has logged heavy workload over the past three days and their xFIP over the last two weeks has climbed to 3.81, a meaningful step back from their season mark.

sections.heading: Schedule and Trends
sections.body: New York is 7-3 over their last ten games with a run differential of plus-18 over the past month. Philadelphia comes in on a getaway day with road splits that trail their home numbers considerably. The Mets are at home for a series opener, a spot where they have been reliable this season.

sections.heading: Putting It Together
sections.body: The offensive advantage is real, the starting pitching matchup tilts New York's way on underlying metrics, and the bullpen situation favors the home side. On balance, the Mets hold the stronger profile in this game and we expect them to win.

sections.heading: Offensive Matchup
sections.body: As we approach the opening game of the season, both the Mets and the Cardinals present offenses that are yet to display their capabilities fully, given the lack of data from the current season. This neutralizes any preliminary analysis based on wRC+, hard-hit%, or barrel%. However, the Mets have consistently invested in strengthening their lineup, with expectations of a high wRC+ once the season unfolds. The Mets' lineup is designed to capitalize on right-handed pitching, which could give them an edge against Jared Shuster, the Cardinals' right-handed starter.

sections.heading: Pitching and Bullpens
sections.body: David Peterson is set to take the mound for the Mets. Although we lack current season metrics, Peterson's potential is significant, with a history of strong FIP numbers that suggest his ERA should positively regress. The Mets' bullpen, with a rating of 70, marginally surpasses the Cardinals' bullpen, rated at 65. This subtle advantage could play a crucial role in late-game scenarios, tipping the balance in the Mets' favor.

sections.heading: Schedule and Trends
sections.body: Both teams are entering their first game of the season, thus neutralizing any recent trends. Playing at home provides the Mets with a comfort level that historically enhances performance metrics, such as xwOBA. The Mets have built a roster that thrives under the pressure of a home crowd, something the Cardinals will have to contend with as they navigate opening day on the road.

sections.heading: Putting It Together
sections.body: Despite the absence of current season data, the Mets' strategic offseason moves have positioned them advantageously. Their potential offensive prowess, combined with a slightly superior bullpen and the home-field advantage, creates a favorable scenario. As the season progresses, expect these underlying strengths to manifest in positive outcomes.
